IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper tackles an important practical gap—Chinese hypernym prediction—by proposing a transductive non-linear framework that leverages unlabeled Baidu Baike data and linguistic rules and reports state-of-the-art gains on FD and the authors’ BK dataset. However, its broader influence is limited by modest generality evidence (authors concede it is not necessarily SOTA for English), a small handcrafted rule set, and no mention of released code or reproducibility artifacts, which reduce immediate uptake by researchers and practitioners. Overall, it is a useful, moderately impactful contribution that could spur follow-up work but is unlikely to fundamentally change the field without stronger validation and reproducibility support. | B(3): The paper tackles a meaningful and under-addressed problem—Chinese hypernym prediction—by combining linear projection models, unlabeled data, and linguistic rules to improve performance on two Chinese datasets (FD and BK), which is practically useful for Chinese KB construction. Its transductive, rule-augmented framework suggests a promising direction for leveraging unlabeled data and language-specific constraints, but the impact is tempered by the limited rule set, reliance on specific Chinese resources, and the authors' own note that it is not necessarily SOTA for English. Overall, this is a useful, domain-relevant contribution that will likely spur follow-up work for multilingual and resource-constrained settings, but it is not transformative across the broader relation extraction field.

However, its broader influence is limited by modest generality evidence (authors concede it is not necessarily SOTA for English), a small handcrafted rule set, and no mention of released code or reproducibility artifacts, which reduce immediate uptake by researchers and practitioners. Overall, it is a useful, moderately impactful contribution that could spur follow-up work but is unlikely to fundamentally change the field without stronger validation and reproducibility support.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful and under-addressed problem—Chinese hypernym prediction—by combining linear projection models, unlabeled data, and linguistic rules to improve performance on two Chinese datasets (FD and BK), which is practically useful for Chinese KB construction. Its transductive, rule-augmented framework suggests a promising direction for leveraging unlabeled data and language-specific constraints, but the impact is tempered by the limited rule set, reliance on specific Chinese resources, and the authors' own note that it is not necessarily SOTA for English. Overall, this is a useful, domain-relevant contribution that will likely spur follow-up work for multilingual and resource-constrained settings, but it is not transformative across the broader relation extraction field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles hypernym prediction (a core lexical-semantic task) with a novel transductive non-linear framework, evaluates on multiple Chinese datasets (FD, a new Baidu-Baike BK) and English benchmarks, and compares against established baselines using standard metrics. From a reproducibility and audience standpoint it mostly meets venue expectations (clear methodology, reported hyperparameters, thorough related work), but the paper has minor scope concerns: its contribution is language-specific (Chinese-focused dataset and a small handcrafted rule set) and code availability is not mentioned, which may limit broad impact and adoption. Overall it targets the right community and uses appropriate evaluation standards, though stronger generalization and public artifacts would strengthen fit. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP task (hypernym/is-a prediction) with a well-motivated transductive non-linear framework, evaluation on Chinese-specific resources (FD, a new Baidu Baike BK dataset) and comparisons to established baselines, so it sits squarely within top-tier NLP venues. The methodology and metrics match community standards and the paper explicitly discusses multilingual applicability and limits (limited handcrafted rules, not SOTA for English), which are appropriate caveats for the audience. My only reservation is scope: the focus on Chinese and a small rule set slightly reduces broad impact compared to more language-agnostic or English-dominant work, so it is a good but not perfect fit.

From a reproducibility and audience standpoint it mostly meets venue expectations (clear methodology, reported hyperparameters, thorough related work), but the paper has minor scope concerns: its contribution is language-specific (Chinese-focused dataset and a small handcrafted rule set) and code availability is not mentioned, which may limit broad impact and adoption. Overall it targets the right community and uses appropriate evaluation standards, though stronger generalization and public artifacts would strengthen fit.

The methodology and metrics match community standards and the paper explicitly discusses multilingual applicability and limits (limited handcrafted rules, not SOTA for English), which are appropriate caveats for the audience. My only reservation is scope: the focus on Chinese and a small rule set slightly reduces broad impact compared to more language-agnostic or English-dominant work, so it is a good but not perfect fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of prior methods (Fu et al. 2014; Wang & He 2016; Li et al. 2015; Mirza & Tonelli 2016; Lenci & Benotto 2012; Santus et al. 2014) and reports standard metrics (Precision/Recall/F) on the FD and a new BK dataset, which indicates largely fair experimental conditions. The authors also explicitly acknowledge limitations (not SOTA for English) and discuss related work thoroughly. However, reproducibility is unclear (code not mentioned) and the summary does not state whether all baselines were reimplemented under identical training/evaluation protocols or whether modern neural baselines were included, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(5): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant prior methods (Fu et al. 2014; Wang & He 2016; Li et al. 2015; Mirza & Tonelli 2016; Lenci & Benotto 2012; Santus et al. 2014) and evaluates on standard Chinese (FD, BK) and English (BLESS, Shwartz) datasets using common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1, PR curves), which indicates fair experimental conditions. The authors explicitly note when their method is not SOTA for English and report hyperparameters and a thorough related-work discussion, and they analyze how their transductive non-linear stage differs from linear projection baselines, so comparisons are comprehensive and well-situated for the paper’s claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of prior methods (Fu et al. 2014; Wang & He 2016; Li et al. 2015; Mirza & Tonelli 2016; Lenci & Benotto 2012; Santus et al. 2014) and reports standard metrics (Precision/Recall/F) on the FD and a new BK dataset, which indicates largely fair experimental conditions. The authors also explicitly acknowledge limitations (not SOTA for English) and discuss related work thoroughly. However, reproducibility is unclear (code not mentioned) and the summary does not state whether all baselines were reimplemented under identical training/evaluation protocols or whether modern neural baselines were included, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of relevant prior methods (Fu et al. 2014; Wang & He 2016; Li et al. 2015; Mirza & Tonelli 2016; Lenci & Benotto 2012; Santus et al. 2014) and evaluates on standard Chinese (FD, BK) and English (BLESS, Shwartz) datasets using common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1, PR curves), which indicates fair experimental conditions. The authors explicitly note when their method is not SOTA for English and report hyperparameters and a thorough related-work discussion, and they analyze how their transductive non-linear stage differs from linear projection baselines, so comparisons are comprehensive and well-situated for the paper’s claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides reasonably comprehensive empirical comparisons across multiple Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and several prior baselines, and reports hyperparameters and standard metrics, which supports its main claims to a moderate degree. However, key reproducibility artifacts are missing from the summary (no code, seeds, runtimes, or compute cost) and it is unclear whether thorough ablations/sensitivity analyses of the transductive/nonlinear components and rule set size were performed. The authors do acknowledge some limitations (limited handcrafted rules; weaker English results), but more systematic failure analyses and release of implementation details would be needed for stronger confidence and full reproducibility. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong on Chinese data (FD and a sizeable BK dataset), compares to relevant baselines, and uses appropriate metrics (precision/recall/F and PR curves); hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. The paper also acknowledges limitations (limited handcrafted rule set and non‑SOTA English results) and evaluates across multiple languages/datasets, but the summary does not indicate dedicated ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of the transductive step, unlabeled data, and rule constraints. Reproducibility is partly supported (hyperparameters reported) but absence of mentioned code and limited rule specification leave minor gaps in full replicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonably comprehensive empirical comparisons across multiple Chinese datasets (FD, BK) and several prior baselines, and reports hyperparameters and standard metrics, which supports its main claims to a moderate degree. However, key reproducibility artifacts are missing from the summary (no code, seeds, runtimes, or compute cost) and it is unclear whether thorough ablations/sensitivity analyses of the transductive/nonlinear components and rule set size were performed. The authors do acknowledge some limitations (limited handcrafted rules; weaker English results), but more systematic failure analyses and release of implementation details would be needed for stronger confidence and full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong on Chinese data (FD and a sizeable BK dataset), compares to relevant baselines, and uses appropriate metrics (precision/recall/F and PR curves); hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. The paper also acknowledges limitations (limited handcrafted rule set and non‑SOTA English results) and evaluates across multiple languages/datasets, but the summary does not indicate dedicated ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of the transductive step, unlabeled data, and rule constraints. Reproducibility is partly supported (hyperparameters reported) but absence of mentioned code and limited rule specification leave minor gaps in full replicability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing improvements on the Chinese FD and BK datasets, with sensible baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, etc.) and reported hyperparameters. Mathematical formulation appears reasonable for the two-stage transductive framework, though no formal proofs are required and none are provided. Methodological concerns remain: the paper does not mention released code or seeds/compute for reproducibility, uses only a small set of handcrafted linguistic rules, and I see no report of statistical significance testing — these are minor but relevant gaps. Overall the empirical evidence largely supports the conclusions, with some reproducibility and robustness details missing. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing improvements on the Chinese FD and BK datasets using a two-stage transductive non-linear framework and comparisons to relevant baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, etc.). The optimization and modeling approach appears internally consistent, but the summary lacks mention of formal proofs and of statistical significance testing for the reported gains. There are modest logical gaps: the claimed generality to other languages is asserted but not empirically demonstrated (English results are explicitly not state-of-the-art), and the method relies on a small handcrafted rule set which the authors acknowledge. Overall the methodological choices and baselines are appropriate, so technical validity holds with minor issues around evidence breadth and statistical reporting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing improvements on the Chinese FD and BK datasets, with sensible baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, etc.) and reported hyperparameters. Mathematical formulation appears reasonable for the two-stage transductive framework, though no formal proofs are required and none are provided. Methodological concerns remain: the paper does not mention released code or seeds/compute for reproducibility, uses only a small set of handcrafted linguistic rules, and I see no report of statistical significance testing — these are minor but relevant gaps. Overall the empirical evidence largely supports the conclusions, with some reproducibility and robustness details missing.

Overall the methodological choices and baselines are appropriate, so technical validity holds with minor issues around evidence breadth and statistical reporting.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; the two-stage methodology (initial linear projection then transductive non-linear learning) is described up front so contributions are easy to grasp. Notation is reported as consistent and hyperparameters are given, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective and informative. Writing quality is generally good, though the authors’ caveat about limited handcrafted rules and the claim about adaptability to other languages leave minor open questions that slightly reduce presentation polish. | B(4): Based on the summary, the paper is well organized with a clear two-stage method (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (transductive non-linear framework integrating linear projections, unlabeled data, and linguistic rules). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (hyperparameters reported, related work covered thoroughly), and the writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues likely remain (e.g., limited discussion of rule coverage and dataset specifics could be expanded), so I rate clarity as very good but not exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion; the two-stage methodology (initial linear projection then transductive non-linear learning) is described up front so contributions are easy to grasp. Notation is reported as consistent and hyperparameters are given, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective and informative. Writing quality is generally good, though the authors’ caveat about limited handcrafted rules and the claim about adaptability to other languages leave minor open questions that slightly reduce presentation polish.

reviewer_b.justification: Based on the summary, the paper is well organized with a clear two-stage method (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (transductive non-linear framework integrating linear projections, unlabeled data, and linguistic rules). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (hyperparameters reported, related work covered thoroughly), and the writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues likely remain (e.g., limited discussion of rule coverage and dataset specifics could be expanded), so I rate clarity as very good but not exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a transductive, non-linear joint-optimization framework that integrates initial linear projection models, unlabeled data, and linguistic-rule constraints to Chinese hypernym prediction — a combination that is not obvious in prior hypernym projection work. However, the components (linear projection, transductive learning, soft linguistic constraints, and non-linear mappings) are largely existing techniques and the problem (hypernym/is-a prediction) is well-studied, so the contribution reads as an incremental but useful methodological combination rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from prior baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, etc.) and demonstrate gains on FD and a new Baidu Baike (BK) dataset, which would inform practitioners focused on Chinese resources but is unlikely to surprise experts as a breakthrough. | B(3): The paper presents a moderately novel combination: it integrates existing linear projection hypernym models with a transductive non-linear optimization over unlabeled pairs and soft linguistic-rule constraints, applied to Chinese datasets (FD and a new Baidu Baike BK set). While the joint transductive formulation and use of confidences and linguistic rules is a non-trivial extension beyond prior projection-only work, the components themselves (linear projection, transductive learning, rule constraints) are established techniques, so the contribution reads as an informed, domain-specific integration rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors clearly differentiate from prior work and adapt methods to Chinese, but an expert would view this as moderate novelty rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a transductive, non-linear joint-optimization framework that integrates initial linear projection models, unlabeled data, and linguistic-rule constraints to Chinese hypernym prediction — a combination that is not obvious in prior hypernym projection work. However, the components (linear projection, transductive learning, soft linguistic constraints, and non-linear mappings) are largely existing techniques and the problem (hypernym/is-a prediction) is well-studied, so the contribution reads as an incremental but useful methodological combination rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from prior baselines (Fu et al., Wang & He, etc.) and demonstrate gains on FD and a new Baidu Baike (BK) dataset, which would inform practitioners focused on Chinese resources but is unlikely to surprise experts as a breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a moderately novel combination: it integrates existing linear projection hypernym models with a transductive non-linear optimization over unlabeled pairs and soft linguistic-rule constraints, applied to Chinese datasets (FD and a new Baidu Baike BK set). While the joint transductive formulation and use of confidences and linguistic rules is a non-trivial extension beyond prior projection-only work, the components themselves (linear projection, transductive learning, rule constraints) are established techniques, so the contribution reads as an informed, domain-specific integration rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors clearly differentiate from prior work and adapt methods to Chinese, but an expert would view this as moderate novelty rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

main_claims: Proposes a transductive non-linear learning framework that integrates initial linear projection models, unlabeled data, and linguistic rules to improve Chinese hypernym prediction.
Outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods for Chinese hypernym prediction on the FD and BK datasets.
Framework is general and can be adapted to English and under-resourced languages, though it is not necessarily state-of-the-art for English.
_summary.methodology: Two-stage method: (1) train initial linear projection models on positive and negative labeled pairs to produce scores and confidences; (2) perform transductive non-linear learning that jointly optimizes final prediction scores for labeled and unlabeled pairs by minimizing errors relative to initial predictions, human labels, and soft linguistic-rule constraints, using nonlinear mappings.
datasets: Baidu Baike corpus (1.2M entity pages, ~1.1B words)
FD (Fu et al., 2014) — 1,391 is-a and 4,294 not-is-a pairs
BK (authors' Baidu Baike dataset) — 3,870 is-a and 3,582 not-is-a pairs
limitations: Only a few handcrafted linguistic rules are used in the current work (authors note limited rule set).
Method is not necessarily state-of-the-art for English hypernym prediction (explicitly stated).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - hypernym prediction / is-a relation extraction

reviewer_a.name: Methodology-Focused Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems in both industry and academia; experience releasing reproducible code and benchmarking across languages and datasets.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, train/validation/test splits, and hyperparameter search details
requires strong baselines, ablations, and statistical significance testing to support empirical claims
looks for reproducibility artifacts (code, seeds, runtimes, compute costs) and implementation details for optimization and model selection
evaluates evaluation metrics and dataset biases, and checks whether improvements generalize across settings
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates papers by scrutinizing experimental rigor, clarity of methods, reproducibility, and whether reported gains are robust and well-supported.

reviewer_b.name: Domain & Impact NLP Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in lexical semantics, relation extraction, and multilingual NLP, with a track record of work on hypernymy induction and knowledge-base construction.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work and practical significance for downstream applications and languages
values theoretical or linguistic insight (e.g., use of rules, language-specific challenges) and plausibility of claims about generality
notices scope and limitations (datasets, languages, annotation quality) and whether contributions advance the research agenda
looks for clear articulation of when and why the method helps, and for connections to related literature across languages
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper based on novelty, contribution to the field, applicability to real-world tasks and languages, and how well it situates itself among prior work.
